message.content: Adversaries may exploit Pluggable Authentication Modules (PAM) to gain unauthorized access or steal credentials by modifying its components. PAM is integral to authentication processes in many systems, managing user credentials through configuration files, libraries, and executables. Commonly targeted components include the domain module, which handles account authentication information in /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow. By patching these components, adversaries can create backdoors, allowing arbitrary values as valid credentials or harvesting plain-text credentials exchanged with PAM. Notable malware like Ebury and Skidmap have demonstrated capabilities to deactivate or replace PAM modules, facilitating unauthorized access and persistent backdoors. Mitigation strategies include monitoring PAM configuration files for unauthorized changes, employing file integrity monitoring, and ensuring robust access controls to prevent unauthorized modifications. Regular audits and updates to PAM-related components can also help in mitigating these risks.
